speaker: interviewer
text: --21st, 1995, Saint Paul, Minnesota, USA. Asking the question, when you were in your room with your friends, playing the records, were you scared at all that you could be found? Or was your mother scared?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. Actually, nobody was scared at the moment. Because that's a thing that you did. And at this moment, in the beginning, we hadn't heard that any group like this would be picked up, that they would bust into the homes or something like that and would pick it up. No, we-- I don't recall that we were scared or anything under those circumstances. We were happy and got together, and we have a lot of fun that way-- a lot of fun that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the next stage in what you call your steps of dehumanization. What occurred?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- well, first of all, the-- the fact that, naturally, that everybody disappeared, little by little, one after the other, but not yet right away at that moment. I think the next step would be important to consider the big change that took place on September 1, 1939, the date that Hitler marched into Poland at the start of World War II. Immediately at that moment, all Jewish people who were working, no matter what they were doing, had to report to a building, like an unemployment center or something like that. And immediately, we were not allowed to go back to our work jobs. We were, in fact in my case-- not in every case. Some people were sent home to report tomorrow again. In my case, I was immediately loaded onto a truck and taken to a work site, out of-- a little bit on the outskirt of the general Berlin, major Berlin. And we were put, in my case, on street work, to build streets and eventually parts of the Autobahn, the Germany highways. And so we were immediately put into slavery work condition. Now, that means, number one, of course, we were allowed to go home. Slavery work condition was that we had to work in this kind of position and receive only half the wages that the normal-- normal-- the, the, the normal German citizen would receive under those circumstances. You see, Hitler-- Hitler had drawn everybody into the war. And so they needed people to work in those kinds of works. The women, by the way, were sent to factories to make ammunition, or to make or repair uniforms, and so on and so forth. But we were there, on the street, working. And not that the employer didn't have to pay the full wages-- he had to pay the full wages-- the only thing was, the system was as follows. Before anything happened, out of the normal full wages, 50% of it he had immediately to take off and sent to Hitler's treasury, to Hitler's coffers. The rest of the 50% that I received, there was first a deduction of the taxes on the whole amount, so that you can imagine there wasn't very much left-- money left over to buy things. On the other hand, that wasn't much of any importance because there wasn't much that you could buy. I told you, you couldn't go anyplace that cost money, to concerts or something like that. And you couldn't buy anything else, rations. Jews, we received only half of the ration cards that the rest of the population received. So money, there was no way that you could spend any money.

speaker: interviewer
text: But tell me again, when you were part of this slave labor force, who were some of the other people that were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were, in my case, men out of all directions of life. In fact, let me give you one particular scene to demonstrate how that looked. We were there, a group of people next to me was a man working, an elderly man. Now, I was-- now I'm talking about 1939, 1940. I was 19 or 20 years old, depending. There was a man working next to me, as I said, an older man. He came to work digging ditches, as we did, so to speak, in a dark, double breasted suit. He looked very, very distinguished, gray-haired, horn-rimmed glasses. And he would work. It was not very handy to handle a shovel. He was smaller than I was, shorter than I was, not a heavy set fellow at all. Was a lightweight man. And he couldn't handle the shovel and the pick axe that we had to swing. And the guards came always and said, hey-- shoved him aside-- look, this is the way you have to do it. And he would swing that pick axe to a big hunk of dirt loose. Took the shovel with a mountain of dirt and threw it a half a mile away. And this is the way you've got to do it. Well, anyway, I must say I was a little embarrassed. By the way, he was not the only one of that type. There were others. In the group of 50 men, in my case, there were others like it. And first of all, I thought he looked so out of place, you know, on this work site, with a dark, elegant-- in my opinion-- looking suit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, that's the whole point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: He turned-- and so I was impatient. And I turned to him, and I said at one time, you know something? I don't understand. Don't you have some working clothes-- as an arrogant young fellow that I probably was. And I don't know what's the matter  with you. Why do you come to work like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was little embarrassed in front of the other people, you know. And so-- and he turned to me after a while and said, you know something? I want you know, young fellow. I know very well what's the matter with me, in very sharp, nice, educated type tone. But I'm wondering, he says, I don't know what's the matter with you. I'm wondering about you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, no, but I don't need to answer any of your questions. But I want to know something, just so you can be satisfied. I-- this is my oldest suit. And this is the way I'm going to come to work, whether you like it or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you see some people say that-- well, let me put it this way very first. One of my colleagues there had noticed that little encounter. And he came to me and he says, hey, don't you know who this man is? I said, no, and what does is matter? Well, of course it matters, but I was too stupid to see it at that time, at that moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, this man is the chief surgeon of the largest hospital in Berlin, the Charite, which still exists-- was in East Berlin as long as it was East Berlin. And besides, he was the, the, the chief of teaching surgery at the university.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was this man with his fine hands, with fine fingers, and his personality and the studies that were made to save lives and to operate on people, he was forced, because he was a Jew, to dig ditches, ditches in the ground, and ruin, of course, his hands, possibly. And, of course, was never, never dug around in his own backyard, I guess, and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: The fact that he came in those clothes, just as an explanation, was very simply that everything was rationed. And Jews received only very, very small ration cards. And for a while, at that time, Jews didn't even receive ration cards for clothing, which the other population received.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even if this man wanted to go and buy himself some dungarees-- in those days we didn't know anything about blue jeans-- he couldn't. There was no way of doing it. And so this was the situation. And, of course, I woke up then  and [INAUDIBLE]. But this was used as propaganda.

speaker: interviewee
text: I noticed suddenly there was a school bus stopping by, not too far from us. It was on the outskirts of Berlin. And it was a nice day. The school was with young school children, maybe third and fourth graders, would come out of the bus with their couple of teachers. And would point over to us. And we noticed they would start laughing and jumping. Well, we didn't know what was going on. It happened again and again. And by that time, I developed a theory. And the fourth time, I recognized there was a teacher from my school with those little kids, a Mr. Baker. And I went, in the evening, to the phone booth. We didn't have any phones. Jews were not allowed to have any phones. Besides, we couldn't have afforded it anyway, so that was no loss to us. But the public phone booth was not allowed to be used by Jews either. So I snuck out though, nevertheless, as I did many, many times on other things. And I would go to the phone booth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, Mr. Baker, this is Henry Oertelt. I don't know if you remember me. And he says on the other end, oh, he says, you, Henry? Yeah, sure, he says. He says, my gosh, how are you doing? You must have tough times right now. I said, yeah, it's not the best. And he was nice. I remember him always as a nice, modified fellow. And he says, look, Henry, I want you to know, this is not going to last. Just hang in there for a while longer. This is going to change. He was very, very nice. And he says, what is it? I said, Mr. Baker, I have to call you and ask you a question. I think I saw you today in such and such area with a school of small children-- class of small children. And he stopped for a moment. He says, Henry, you were in that group that we saw working there? I says, yes. I said, yes. I said, would you mind to tell me what that's all about? I didn't really need it. I knew already.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, Henry, I'll tell you. What I have to do, I hate every moment. But I have to do it. And he says, you probably know that if I don't do what I am being told, I will lose my job. And I have a wife and children. And, of course, he was absolutely correct. I knew that. People that would not obey the orders, teachers would not follow the track that was described to them, would just be fired. And would not only be fired, would be anti-state, would be black-marked forever. And so I understood this. And he says, well, what I will tell you what's going on. Like, for instance, on Friday night at school, it says, well, on Monday morning-- over the speaker system-- a grades so-and-so-- it's always the younger grades-- we will have a bus for you ready on Monday morning. And we will show you what a bunch of Jews really look like. You see, by that time, everybody had heard the propaganda about Jews. And in the classrooms, by the way, were bad pictures about Jews and compared with the Aryan master race pictures and on and so forth. So kids were already brainwashed negativity about Jews. If they have never had met any Jewish people, person-to-person, now they get the education of what Jews are like. And steadily repeated, of course it sinks in eventually. And so now-- and these buses-- now we will show you what a bunch of Jews really look like. And so they go onto the buses now, and are being loaded up in front of people like that, a motley-looking group, most of them like this doctor, the professor that I am talking about. And I was a handy fellow with my work, with my hands, as I say, building furniture. I was used to carrying lumber, carrying furniture, besides my drawing. And so this, this physically work didn't-- I was, you know-- so it didn't make any difference to me. But there were some old guys that had never had the shovel in their hands. And even a simple thing as a shovel and a pick axe, you have to know how to handle that, too, particularly when it was there like quote, unquote, 'professionally.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyways, so these kids now are standing in front of the bus and looking at this motley-looking group. And the teacher is suppose say, now look at these miserable looking characters over there. Don't you see? This is what Mr. Baker tells me. Don't you see, they don't even know handle a pick axe. Doesn't it look funny though? Look at it. Look at this one guy in the middle there. The shovel isn't even full, and he can't even throw it very far onto the wagon where he's supposed to. And these kids are laughing at this motley-looking group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look at what they are dressed in. They don't look anything like the hard hats that you normally see in workplaces like that. There were no steam shovels, nothing automatic where these groups were working, all the groups were working. And so they look really odd, out of it completely. And now, these kids are going home and will know from now on what Jews are really look like. That they don't even-- they are so stupid, they don't even know how to handle a shovel and a pick axe. And these kids are growing up now with that picture in their mind forever-- the power of propaganda, the power of dehumanization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how are you feeling at this point? For so many generations, I suppose there were Jews in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, you know, here you have the Fatherland, where a lot of Jews gave their lives in World War I. And here now is, I guess, a complete betrayal of your fatherland against you. How did you feel at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you feel-- of course you feel quite miserable at this moment. But also, if you are a person that has eternal hope and if you're an optimist, you know and you hear from all sides. Just like Mr. Baker says, just hang in there, it won't last much longer. And this is what you feel all along. We have to take it for the time being, and it will change. The war is going on. We were convinced that the Allies would defeat the Germans. We were absolutely convinced about it. Just, we didn't realize it would take that long. We thought, my gosh, look at it. By now we hear, once in awhile, of some loss of positions by the Nazi forces. And we figure, oh, now it starts, you know. Give it another two or three months and the war will be over. Now, that is already five years before the end, you know-- five, six years before the end that I'm talking about. So this is the only thing. You did not dwell too much on your misery and disappointment. Although you talk about it. For instance, my uncle was a World War I hero. He had the highest decoration, the Iron Cross First Class. He had that highest decoration, and he was an enthusiastic, first war won, defending his fatherland, an enthusiastic soldier. And got that thing, which Hitler, by the way, claimed later on, the Jews received those things only by deceit. And he had, by the way, had to deliver it back, eventually, to the Nazis. And so, yes, you fear, but you don't dwell too much on it. You're not the happiest guy. You know, you can't go here, can't go there, and you don't like it. And you learn to live with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're 19. You're a teenager. You're involved in this situation. What about any kind of a social life? Were you just tired when you came home from work? Or were you just glad that nobody was bothering you? And what was your brother doing at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother was always-- I remember my brother only busy over the books and studying, studying, studying. And he is a genius.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why didn't have to get involved with this kind of activity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, oh, yeah, eventually then, yeah-- once the war started, yes, he was, he was, he was kept at the university a year longer. And the only reason was that he had so many friends among the professors, because they admired him so much as a genius, that he was the very last Jew that finally had to be thrown out of the university, too. But eventually, he was also involved in the slavery work. And he was-- just imagine that-- he was ordered to work for a company that-- a moving company that moved people around. So he had to carry and schlep furniture and all this stuff. And he was in pretty good shape because he was a very, very good athlete. He was boxing in the Jewish club, Maccabi-- Maccabiah. Maccabi, we said in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was trained in what they called in those days, Jujitsu. And he was also in lithe athletics. He was, during one year, the champion of the gymnasium, they called it at that time, which is sort of like a high school. And so he was very well physical, in physical shape. And he could carry the furniture with the best of them around there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about some of the Jewish organizations that you had belonged to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I belonged to a youth group. It's called the [GERMAN]. The exact translation would be the, the brotherhood, sisterhood of German Jewish youth. And so this was sort of like a Pathfinder connected outfit. We sang all the Pathfinder songs, which the Scouts, Scout movement. And we were, until later and for a time, for why we were even allowed and had special permission to be, to going, to go on outings and so on and so forth, which eventually was prohibited. No Jew was allowed to leave the city. And so we had some fun with that. And, of course, our friendship groups, we were constantly together, either here in this house, or that house, or that. And so we had actually a lot of fun. And I think, if it wasn't so harsh that you were not allowed to go anyplace, it is really a good recipe for many, many youth groups, if they would cut down on all these mall visitings, and movies, and this stuff, and that stuff. If they would start to get together with their friends in their houses and--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's sort of like a captive situation there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start to really realize that, that it's not going to go on for just a few more months, that there's some real imminent danger that's about to befall you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, I have to go back for a moment. Even though we were not allowed to go places, I still did. I went to soccer games. I went to concerts. I went to the movie theater. And I came home after curfew. And, of course, my poor mother-- and in those days, I didn't see it that way-- my poor mother just went out of here mind because it was prohibited. And here and there we found out that somebody was caught and never seen again, that did the same thing. And so when I went out and she says, well, you're going to be home-- if it was, oh, you're going to be home before 9 o'clock. I said, Mom, I don't think so. I don't know. Don't worry about me. And I suppose every parent and every youngster goes through that. You know, Ma, don't worry. You know, I am not a baby anymore. Nothing will happen to me. I'm going to watch out. And so then, a close friend of mine disappeared. And I had an answer for that too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Robert Shapiro, that time. So he, he disappeared. And I said-- and my mother said, now, you see what happened to him? And I said, Mom, I know he's a nice guy, but he is kind of careless. I am not that way. You know, I am watching out. I know, I can smell a Gestapo guy behind me, practically. And I am watching out. Which I did, I really watched out very, very carefully. And this guy was kind of like easy-go lucky guy. And I don't know what happened, exactly the details, except he disappeared, was picked up-- some people said-- in some place that he wasn't supposed to be. And he was never seen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you have to start wearing a star?

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, that came to the eyes and ears of the Nazis, that some of those Jews would not obey their orders and would go places anyways. And so they came up with a-- as they always came up with some idea to put the thumb screws tighter-- they said, now, regretfully, not every Jew looks like what we think a Jew is supposed to look like.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know what that means anyways. What are Jews supposed to look like? And so we're going to make darn sure, from now on, that everybody will know from a mile away that this person is a Jew. And they came out with this little item here, this little star.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an original. This is not one made up. And I keep, therefore-- that's why I keep it in plastic, so anybody can touch it if they want to. As you can see, this star is in ugly colors, like Halloween colors, and has a weird inscription inside that looks-- the hieroglyphics look strange to some people. Hitler and his henchmen tried to tell people, for years already, these Jews are strange people. They are so strange. And now, look at this here. They know the Jews have to wear this kind of thing. It's hieroglyphics that, I think they tried to imitate, somehow, the Hebrew writing. And the letters are supposed to represent-- are representing-- J-U-D-E, which is the word for Jew. And so this is made out of fine cloth. And with it came the order that this has to be sewn on your left side of whatever outer garment you wear, in tight stitches, like one millimeter apart from each other. And now we, we were stopped on the streets, sometimes to see and checked if these stitches are not-- I know of a person that had a corner loose. And some eyewitnesses saw that he was stopped. The Gestapo guy grabbed the corner, tore the star off, and another Gestapo guy grabbed the guy by the neck and shoved him into the waiting black limousine there. So another--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not I, a friend of mine. But this man-- I knew the person that it happened to. And this person was taken away and was never seen again. In other words, this was a situation-- you're talking about danger and being frightened-- a loose corner of this dumb star could cost you your life, as it did in this case, and in many other cases I know, too. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember sewing that onto your clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, sure. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are you thinking at the time that you're sewing this star on, how ridiculous this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly, it's a ridiculous thing. But you don't know, and you are now concerned, maybe yeah-- this is what my mother said, you know something, as much as I hate this thing, I am glad about it. This at least now keeps you home and out of danger, because I don't think you dare to go after curfew in a place with this thing. Now, by the way, since this had to stitch on so many stitches, you couldn't-- as I'm sitting here in this shirt-- sew it on here. If I wore a jacket, it would have to be on the other side of the jacket. You couldn't just always take the one off and put it and sew it back on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in other words, you bought a whole bunch of them. And they were not exactly cheap. And, of course, you know where you bought them. You bought them at your friendly neighborhood Nazi store. That's where you bought them. So it was-- How much were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly anymore. No, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you bought a bunch of them. But you see, I got tired of this thing. And eventually, I got an idea. I took a tin can, cut it open, flattened it out, took my tin snips, and cut out the form of that star, and took one those stars and glued it onto that metal piece. And now I-- and then, I took my trusting soldering iron. And I had a long pin. And I soldered the pin on the back of that metal piece, with the star in front there now. And that was just a pin with a long, pointy pin. And this, now, I could easily stick onto anything. Yes, it was supposed to be sewed on. And so, of course, I figured, so it looks tight, I bent the corners inward like that. So when it was on the front-- on a piece of clothing, it looked-- at least from a few steps away-- like it is tight on. I never thought of the possibility that somebody stops me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were never stopped?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happens only to other people, you know, everything else. But in my case, fortunately that's what happened. I was never stopped. I had very ugly scene, and I feel sorry now about that, of course. As a youngster, you know, you wanted to be free. It was a sort of a resistance. There's no questions about it. How could these guys dare tell me, people, my family that was for generations in Germany-- I'm at least as much German as anybody else-- how can they dare to tell me that I can't go to my beloved concerts or my beloved soccer games. And so, therefore, I did that with that pin. And so, with the help of a briefcase-- that almost every man carries over there and carried over there and still does, I see-- I covered this up. When it came to a point to take it off, and took it off secretly. And so I had, I had my own gimmick, very, very easily trained. I was very quick with this thing, took it off, had the brief case in front. And it started-- when I would take it off-- slid right into the side pocket of my coat. And I reversed the procedure when I came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. How long did you work at the slave labor? And do that kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the slave labor, I worked until I was finally picked up to be sent to the camp. Let me tell you. It was about in 1943-- in March of 1943-- when Hitler announced, in big headlines in the newspapers and over the radio, congratulations to the German population. We now can tell you that 90% of the Jews of Germany and other areas have been-- and use the word-- 'evacuated.'

speaker: interviewer
text: 90%. The great mathematician that I was, I figured, my gosh, 10%? I belong to the 10% that are still here. And we also heard now news about Hitler's successes-- or lack of successes on the fronts. And we figured, my gosh, now the Allies really having starting to return.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was just a time, I guess, when Hitler was-- his troops were thrown back from the Russian front, somewhat. And so we figured, it can only take a few months, really, and the war is over. And so by now, we figured, since he has those problems, and he announced 10% of people are still there-- of Jews still there-- he has other problems, maybe now, as miserable as our life is at this point, but it's good enough to hang in there yet for a while longer. We knew by that time that being sent to the concentration camp is definitely much, much worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you've heard about the concentration camps now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes, yes, yes. So that's how we hung in there. Concentration camps, there was one right next to Berlin Oranienburg, Sachsenhausen as it is known at. And so we figured to hang in there is the best thing. That was now in March of 1943. Yet, people knew about concentration camps. There was a saying in Berlin that, of people that didn't want to make remarks, anti-Nazi or criticizing Hitler or anybody-- well, I better not say too much or else I'm being sent to the concentration camp, to Oranienburg was actually, the Berliners used to be afraid of.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: People knew. I must-- I grant that they did not know the details that were going on in the camps. But they knew that much, that this is a place they don't want to be sent to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now one final question before we change the tapes here. I'm still trying to kind of put together your life between the beginning of the war and like, now, 1943. Did you work at the slave labor camps-- excuse me-- a slave labor that whole time? Or what else were you involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, until World War II started, September 1, I worked-- I just had finished in my apprenticeship. I was now a full-fledged designer and furniture builder. And from that time on, though, I worked at the slave labor conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the slave labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you want to change it?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How much time do we have left on there?
